attributes.stem:
A 22-year-old man is brought to the Emergency Department by his friends after becoming unresponsive at a nightclub. His friends report he was drinking alcohol and may have taken some recreational drugs, but they are unsure what he consumed. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears unwell with a Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS) score of 8 (E2 V2 M4). His vital signs are: pulse rate 58/min, blood pressure 100/60 mmHg, respiratory rate 10/min, SpO2 94% on room air, and temperature 36.5°C. His pupils are 3 mm and reactive. There are no signs of trauma. He has no needle track marks.

A blood gas shows:

  • pH 7.30 (7.35-7.45)

  • pCO2 50 mmHg (35-45)

  • pO2 70 mmHg (75-100)

  • HCO3- 24 mmol/L (22-26)

  • Lactate 1.5 mmol/L (0.5-1.6)

Naloxone administration does not improve his condition. After about two hours of supportive care, he begins to regain consciousness rapidly.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely causative agent in this patient's overdose?
attributes.explanation:
The clinical presentation in this case is most consistent with an overdose of GHB (gamma-hydroxybutyrate). GHB is a central nervous system depressant often used recreationally in nightclub settings. Key features supporting this diagnosis include:

1. Sudden onset of decreased consciousness (GCS 8) without focal neurological signs.

2. Respiratory depression (respiratory rate 10/min, SpO2 94%, elevated pCO2).

3. Mild bradycardia (pulse rate 58/min) and hypotension (100/60 mmHg).

4. Lack of response to naloxone, ruling out opioid overdose.

5. Rapid recovery within a few hours, which is characteristic of GHB's short half-life.

Cocaine overdose typically presents with agitation, tachycardia, hypertension, and hyperthermia, which are not seen in this patient. Cocaine is a stimulant, unlike the depressant effects observed here.

MDMA (ecstasy) overdose usually causes hyperthermia, tachycardia, hypertension, and agitation. The patient's normal temperature and depressed vital signs make this unlikely.

Ketamine can cause a dissociative state, but typically does not cause significant respiratory depression. It also tends to increase heart rate and blood pressure, unlike the presentation here.

Methamphetamine, like cocaine, is a stimulant that would be expected to cause agitation, tachycardia, hypertension, and hyperthermia. The depressant effects seen in this patient are inconsistent with methamphetamine use.

The management of GHB overdose is primarily supportive, focusing on airway protection and ventilatory support if needed. Most patients recover rapidly due to GHB's short half-life, as seen in this case. It's important to note that polydrug use is common in recreational settings, so clinicians should remain vigilant for signs of other substance use or complications.
learningPoints: GHB overdose typically presents with sudden onset of drowsiness, confusion, and respiratory depression.
GHB has a short half-life, often leading to rapid recovery within 1-3 hours.
Naloxone is not effective in reversing GHB overdose, unlike opioid overdoses.
references: Le JK, Richards JR. Gamma-Hydroxybutyrate Toxicity. [Updated 2023 Aug 8]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK430781/

Busardò FP, Jones AW. GHB pharmacology and toxicology: acute intoxication, concentrations in blood and urine in forensic cases and treatment of the withdrawal syndrome. Curr Neuropharmacol. 2015 Jan;13(1):47-70. doi: 10.2174/1570159X13666141210215423. PMID: 26074743; PMCID: PMC4462042.

attributes.description: Apply, analyze, evaluate, and create (implementing, organizing, and critiquing information)
